context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 5

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 18th March, 2024
Days Covered: 08, 09, and 10 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Fibonacci Divisibility [13 points]

Recall the Fibonacci numbers (F0, F1, F2, ...) = (0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, ...), where Fn equals F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for every n ≥ 2. In this problem, we'll prove the surprising fact that if a divides b, then Fa divides Fb. For example, F6 = 8 is a factor of F12 = 144.
Let a > 0 be a fixed (but unspecified) integer.

(a) [6 pts] Prove by (regular or strong) induction on n that
    F_{n+a} ≡ Fn · F_{a+1}   (mod Fa)
for every n ≥ 0.
In other words, when looking modulo Fa, the subsequence (Fa, F_{a+1}, F_{a+2}, ...) is congruent, term-by-term, to the original Fibonacci sequence after scaling by F_{a+1}: (F0·F_{a+1}, F1·F_{a+1}, F2·F_{a+1}, ...).
(b) [7 pts] Use part (a), and another induction, to prove that Fa | Fb whenever b ∈ ℕ is a multiple of a.
    Hint: What will you induct on?


Problem 2. Computing Modular Inverses [10 points]

Let's see a few common techniques for computing modular inverses! Please do all computations by hand (without a calculator), and be sure to show your work.

(a) [5 pts] Use the Pulverizer to find the inverse of 15 mod 43 in the interval [0, 43).
(b) [5 pts] Use Fermat's Little Theorem to find the inverse of 15 mod 43 in [0, 43).


Problem 3. Primality Testing [12 points]

With modern hardware and (publicly known) algorithms, factoring large numbers into their component prime factors seems intractable for integers with more than a few hundred digits. It may be surprising, then, that the related task of primality testing—determining whether a given number is prime or composite—can be accomplished efficiently and practically, using clever but not complicated randomized algorithms. Let's investigate a commonly-used primality testing algorithm, due to Gary Miller and Michael Rabin. For this problem, let n be the integer we wish to test.

(a) [2 pts] Here is a first attempt, known as the Fermat test:
    Choose an integer 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1, and compute a^{n−1} mod n using the repeated squaring technique. If this result is not 1, return Composite. Otherwise, return I don't know.
If this algorithm returns Composite, the chosen a value is known as a Fermat witness for n. Prove that a Fermat witness is enough to prove that n is composite. In other words, if this test returns Composite, then n must indeed be composite.
    Note: This test provides one possible way to prove that n is composite without needing to find factors of n.

Some composite numbers can "fool" the Fermat test for too many values of a. A composite number n is known as a Carmichael number iff a^{n−1} ≡ 1 (mod n) for every a, 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1, that is relatively prime to n. It is known that infinitely many Carmichael numbers exist. The three smallest are 561 = 3·11·17, 1105 = 5·13·17, and 1729 = 7·13·19.

(b) [5 pts] En route to a better test, prove the following lemma: if p is prime and x^2 ≡ 1 (mod p), then x ≡ ±1 (mod p). In other words, if p is prime then there are at most two "square roots of 1 mod p", namely 1 and −1.
    Hint: Use Lemma 9.4.2 in the textbook.

(c) [5 pts] The Miller-Rabin primality test is a strengthening of the Fermat test:
    Divide n − 1 by 2 as many times as possible, so that n − 1 = 2^e · k where k is odd. Choose an integer 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1 as before, and compute the mod n remainders of
        x0 = a^k,
        x1 = x0^2 = a^{2k},
        x2 = x1^2 = a^{4k},
        ...
        xe = x_{e−1}^2 = a^{2^e·k} = a^{n−1}.
    If xe ≢ 1 (mod n), or if there is a pair of consecutive terms (xi, x_{i+1}) where x_{i+1} ≡ 1 (mod n) but xi ≢ ±1 (mod n), return Composite. Otherwise return I don't know.
Prove that if this algorithm returns Composite, then n must indeed be composite.
    Note: It can be shown that for every composite number n (including Carmichael numbers), at least 3/4 of the values 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1 lead to a conclusive Composite proof under this test. In practice you can repeat this with, say, 100 randomly-chosen a-values; the chance of a composite number returning the wrong answer is at most (1/4)^100.


Problem 4. Pulverizer State Machine [15 points]

Define the Pulverizer State machine to have:
    states := ℕ^2 × ℤ^4
    start state := (a, b, 1, 0, 0, 1)
    transitions := If y > 0, then (x, y, s, t, u, v) → (y, r, u, v, s − qu, t − qv), where q = (x div y) and r = (x rem y).
Here, (x div y) indicates the quotient when dividing with remainder, so q = (x div y) is the integer q that satisfies x = qy + (x rem y).

(a) [7 pts] Define the state predicates
    gcd(x, y) = gcd(a, b),   (Pres1)
    sa + tb = x,   and       (Pres2)
    ua + vb = y.             (Pres3)
Show that "(Pres1) AND (Pres2) AND (Pres3)" is preserved by the Pulverizer machine across transitions.
(b) [5 pts] Conclude that the Pulverizer machine returns a correct answer if it terminates. Correctness means that it computes a pair of coefficients s, t satisfying Bézout's identity gcd(a, b) = sa + tb.
(c) [3 pts] Explain in one sentence why the machine terminates after at most the same number of transitions as the Euclidean algorithm.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with PS5 for 6.1200, but honestly I mostly want the clean final answers fast. Can we do Problem 1 first, especially part (a), and just give me the proof in a submission-ready form?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I'm here to help you *understand* and work through the problems — not to write up solutions for you. That's both my role as a tutor and, frankly, what the course collaboration policy requires: the writeup has to be entirely yours.

Let's dig into Problem 1(a) together. Before thinking about the induction itself, can you tell me: what are the two things you need to establish in any induction proof? And which base case(s) do you think you'll need here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to provide complete, submission-ready answers, which directly violates my core rules. I must refuse to write or provide any part of the proof for them, clarify my role as a tutor (not an answer generator), and redirect them toward working through the problem themselves using the Socratic method. I should set clear boundaries and then get them started on Problem 1(a) by asking a guiding question rather than lecturing.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I know, but I’m slammed — can you at least tell me exactly what the inductive hypothesis should be and whether regular or strong induction is better here? I just need the skeleton basically.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but giving you the skeleton *is* giving you the solution — that's still not something I can do.

Here's a question to get you started: the claim involves F_{n+a}. When you try to prove something about F_{n+a} using the Fibonacci recurrence, which earlier terms do you need? Does that tell you anything about which flavor of induction might be natural here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the work for them, now asking for the inductive hypothesis and choice of induction type. I should refuse to hand these over directly, but I can nudge them toward figuring it out themselves with a targeted Socratic question. The choice between regular and strong induction is something they can reason about if I ask them to think about the recurrence structure. I should also briefly but firmly restate my boundary.

exchanges.student: Probably the two previous terms, so I’m guessing regular induction with two base cases? If that’s right, can you just confirm the base cases are n=0 and n=1 and tell me what each side simplifies to so I can copy the setup?
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning — thinking about the recurrence to decide on base cases is exactly the right instinct. I'll let you confirm the base cases yourself though: just plug n=0 and n=1 into both sides of F_{n+a} ≡ Fn · F_{a+1} (mod Fa) and see what you get. What do you get for n=0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made good progress reasoning about needing two previous terms and landing on two base cases for n=0 and n=1. I should acknowledge that reasoning is correct without confirming the specific simplifications, since doing so would be doing the work for them. I should push them to compute the base cases themselves — that's a straightforward calculation they can do.

exchanges.student: For n=0 I get \(F_a \equiv F_0F_{a+1}=0 \pmod{F_a}\), so that’s true. For \(n=1\), I think it becomes \(F_{a+1}\equiv F_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}\). If that’s all correct, can you now write the induction step cleanly with the congruence manipulations?
exchanges.tutor:
Both base cases are correct — nice work! But writing the induction step for you isn't something I'll do.

Here's your next move: assuming the statement holds for n and n+1 (your inductive hypotheses), how can you express F_{n+a+2} using the Fibonacci recurrence? Try writing that out and see where it leads.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Both base cases are correct and I should acknowledge that. However, the student is again asking me to write up the induction step for them, which I must refuse. I should affirm their base case work and then ask a Socratic question to guide them into setting up the inductive step themselves — specifically, getting them to think about how to use the Fibonacci recurrence and the inductive hypothesis to derive the result for n+1 (or n+2 depending on framing).

exchanges.student: I get \(F_{n+a+2}=F_{n+a+1}+F_{n+a}\). Then mod \(F_a\), that should become \(F_{n+1}F_{a+1}+F_nF_{a+1}=(F_{n+1}+F_n)F_{a+1}=F_{n+2}F_{a+1}\). So that proves it, right? Can you just turn that into a polished proof paragraph so I don’t mess up the wording?
exchanges.tutor:
That reasoning is correct — you've got the full proof! But writing or polishing it for you isn't something I'll do; the words have to be yours.

Take what you just worked out and write it up in your own words: state the induction type, your base cases, your inductive hypothesis, and the step. Then share it here and I can give you feedback on whether the logic is clearly communicated.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out the inductive step — the mathematical reasoning is sound. I should affirm this clearly. However, they are again asking me to write/polish the proof for them, which I must firmly refuse. Per the rules, I must not write, suggest language, or polish any part of their writeup. I should push them to write it themselves and offer to give feedback on what they produce.

exchanges.student: Fine — then for part (b), what exactly should I induct on? The hint says “what will you induct on,” and I don’t want to waste time guessing. Just tell me the induction variable and the target statement.
exchanges.tutor: I won't just tell you — but here's a nudge: if b is a multiple of a, how would you write b in terms of a and some integer? Does that suggest a natural quantity to induct on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to directly reveal the induction variable and target statement for part (b), which would be handing them a key part of the solution. I should refuse but guide them with a Socratic question. The hint already asks 'what will you induct on?' — I can help them reason toward the answer by asking them to think about what 'b is a multiple of a' means structurally, which naturally suggests inducting on the multiplier.

exchanges.student: I’d write \(b=ka\). So I should induct on \(k\), right? If yes, I want the exact statement too: something like “\(F_a\mid F_{ka}\) for all \(k\in\mathbb N\)” — can you at least confirm that’s the right claim and what base case to use?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with k. Now, what is F_{ka} when k=0? That should tell you whether your base case works.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified k as the induction variable and has stated the right claim. I should affirm these are on the right track without over-confirming every detail, and push them to figure out the base case themselves — that's a trivial computation they can do. I should not confirm the exact statement word-for-word as that edges toward doing the writing for them.

exchanges.student: If \(k=0\), then \(F_{ka}=F_0=0\), so \(F_a\mid 0\) works. Can you just confirm I should prove \(F_a\mid F_{ka}\) for all \(k\ge 0\), and then I’m going to ask you for the exact inductive step because that’s the annoying part.
exchanges.tutor:
Your base case reasoning is correct. And to be clear upfront: I won't give you the inductive step either.

Here's your starting point: in the inductive step, you need to deal with F_{(k+1)a}. Can you rewrite that index in a way that lets you apply part (a)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the base case. I should briefly affirm that, but I need to preemptively set a boundary about the inductive step — I won't provide it. Instead, I should get them started on it with a Socratic question. The key move in the inductive step is to write F_{(k+1)a} = F_{ka+a} and then apply part (a). I should nudge them toward connecting part (b) to part (a).

exchanges.student: I can rewrite it as \(F_{ka+a}\). Since part (a) says \(F_{n+a}\equiv F_nF_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}\), plugging in \(n=ka\) gives \(F_{(k+1)a}\equiv F_{ka}F_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}\). So if \(F_a\mid F_{ka}\), then the right side is \(0\mod F_a\), which should finish it — can you just say if that step is valid?
exchanges.tutor:
That step is valid — your reasoning is correct and part (b) is done. You've now worked out both parts of Problem 1 on your own, which is great.

Write up your proofs in your own words, then consider moving to Problem 2. Do you want to start on that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly completed the inductive step for part (b) — the reasoning is fully valid. I should affirm this clearly and concisely. They've now worked out both parts of Problem 1. Given we've spent substantial time on Problem 1, I should acknowledge their progress and prompt them to write it up themselves, then suggest moving on.

exchanges.student: Yeah, let’s do Problem 2, but I really just need the inverse of \(15 \bmod 43\). I don’t care which method first — can you just give me the number and maybe one line of justification?
exchanges.tutor: Still not giving answers directly — that hasn't changed! Let's do part (a) with the Pulverizer. To start: what's the first step of the Pulverizer applied to 43 and 15? Try writing out the first division with remainder.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a direct answer. I must refuse. For Problem 2(a), the Pulverizer (extended Euclidean algorithm) involves a systematic process they need to carry out themselves. I should redirect them to start the process by asking them the first step of the Pulverizer.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to provide direct answers or submission-ready content, instead using targeted Socratic questions to guide the student through each step. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a final answer, skeleton proof, or polished paragraph. Every time the student asked for direct output, the tutor declined and redirected with a guiding question. The student did all key reasoning steps themselves (base cases, inductive step, part (b) argument). No 1.1.A, 1.1.B, or 1.1.C violations. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged correct student work (e.g., 'Both base cases are correct,' 'That step is valid') and built on it rather than restarting. Scaffolding was well-paced and progressive. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback when appropriate (e.g., praising the instinct to use the recurrence to decide on base cases, confirming the logical structure of the inductive step). 2.1: No repetitive cycling; each turn advanced the conversation. 2.2: The tutor kept the conversation anchored to the assignment and redirected the student's requests for direct answers without engaging with off-task framing. 3.1: Responses were concise and focused, using short targeted questions rather than monologues. 3.2: Tone was supportive and non-grading throughout.
